REAL-TALK is a new discourse elicitation battery designed to support person-centered care across the continuum of aphasia recovery through co-designed stimuli and procedures, graded task complexity, optional supports, and digital deployment. Guided by the Exploration, Preparation, Implementation, Sustainment (EPIS) framework, the present work is positioned within the Exploration and Preparation stages. Phase I included two virtual focus groups and a follow-up confirmatory survey with nine adults with chronic post-stroke aphasia. Participants called for discourse tasks that are contemporary, colorful, culturally diverse, personally meaningful, clearly explained, and tiered by difficulty. They emphasized the importance of topic choice, clinician-guided personalization, feedback, reflection over time, and stronger connections to everyday communication. Across genres, participants favored inference-based picture description prompts, real-world picture sequences, personal narratives, and procedural tasks linked to activities of daily living, while traditional story-retell tasks such as Cinderella were generally viewed as outdated, overly complex, and less motivating. These findings provide a community-informed blueprint for Preparation-stage development of REAL-TALK, including candidate stimulus creation, iterative review with PWA and speech-language pathologists, within-genre difficulty tiering, and later clinical validation.

articles.title: From Foundations to Frontiers: Fit-for-Purpose Discourse Science
articles.authors: Brielle C Stark
articles.abstract:
Purpose: This clinical focus piece responds to discussion at the 2026 International Cognitive Communication Disorders Conference (ICCDC), where participants highlighted a productive tension that has shaped discourse research for decades. Canonical discourse elicitation tasks (e.g., Cinderella retell) remain valuable because they support historical continuity, cross-study comparison, and shared datasets, yet they do not always capture the discourse constructs most relevant for all populations or clinical purposes. Discourse analysis has long been central to the assessment and treatment of neurogenic and cognitive-communication communication disorders, and canonical tasks have shaped decades of research by yielding replicable, analyzable, and linguistically rich language samples.
Method: This clinical focus article is a narrative review that draws on the author’s personal experience as a translational researcher and deep knowledge of the field.
Results and Conclusion: This clinical focus piece examines what canonical tasks revealed about language breakdown, why they succeeded within their original scope, where they may be inappropriate, and why and when they should still be retained. Building on discussion from ICCDC, this piece argues that the field does not simply need “new tasks,” but rather a principled framework for deciding when canonical tasks remain useful, when they should be adapted, and when new tasks, methods, and metrics are warranted. A fit-for-purpose discourse science is proposed, in which discourse tasks and outcome measures are selected because they are appropriate, robust, and aligned with the purpose, population, and construct of interest, while also integrating psychometric rigor, participatory design, and thoughtful use of technologies such as natural language processing and perceptual ratings. This clinical focus piece also offers practical tables summarizing elicitation methods, suitable measures, key features of strong discourse assessment, clinically feasible implementation principles, and example scenarios for choosing canonical and non-canonical tasks in research and practice.

articles.title: SAS-1: A single-item version of the Smartphone Addiction Scale
articles.authors: Jay A. Olson; Ellen Langer; Loren Martin
articles.abstract: The digital age has brought increasing concern about the negative effects of smartphones, which has led to dozens of measures of problematic smartphone use. Almost all of these measures, however, may be too long for contexts such as large-scale surveys. Here, we introduce a single-item measure that probes agreement with the statement: ‘I am addicted to my smartphone’. Across 10,786 participants aged 5 to 89 from 149 countries, 37% agreed or strongly agreed with this statement. Their agreement strongly correlated (r = .65 to .70) with scores on the short version of the Smartphone Addiction Scale (SAS-SV), the most widely used scale in the field. Younger women had the highest scores on both measures and tended to somewhat underestimate their addiction on the single-item scale. Overall, our results suggest that most people have an accurate self-assessment of their problematic smartphone use. This new 1-item scale (SAS-1) can therefore serve as a brief measure of problematic smartphone use when space is limited.

articles.title: Built to Last: Online operant conditioning induces nocebo expectancies resistant to modification in chronic pain patients and pain-free participants
articles.authors: NA; Izabela Łaska; Daryna Rubanets; Joanna Kłosowska; Przemysław Babel; Elżbieta A. Bajcar
articles.abstract: Digital environments increasingly shape health beliefs and treatment expectancies, including negative expectancies that may contribute to nocebo effects. This study examined whether nocebo expectancies can be induced online through operant conditioning, reduced through verbal modeling, and whether these processes differ between chronic pain patients and pain-free participants. Participants were assigned to one of four groups: experimental pain-free, experimental chronic pain, control pain-free, and control chronic pain. They watched videos of a model receiving pain stimuli either with or without a placebo. During the induction phase, participants rated how much pain they expected the model to experience after each stimulus. In the experimental groups, responses indicating higher expected pain in the placebo condition were reinforced by the experimenter, whereas reinforcement was noncontingent in the control groups. During the modification phase, participants observed the model receiving pain stimuli and were shown ratings allegedly provided by other participants, suggesting that others had rated the model’s pain in the placebo condition as lower than in the control condition; in the control groups, these ratings were presented randomly. After each phase and again after a 3-day delay, participants rated expected pain in the placebo and control conditions. Online operant conditioning effectively induced nocebo expectancies, which were not reduced by online verbal modeling and persisted after a 3-day delay. No significant differences emerged between chronic pain and pain-free participants. These findings suggest that brief expert-delivered online feedback may be sufficient to shape nocebo expectancies that are resistant to subsequent modification.

articles.title: A test of seasonal effects on cognition in tournament chess players
articles.authors: Shaheed Azaad; Yoshihisa Kashima; Nick Haslam
articles.abstract: Research has revealed considerable seasonal variation in human behaviour and emotion. However, whether cognitive performance is similarly seasonal remains an open question. While some studies have found a performance advantage in summer compared to winter, others have found either the reverse effect or no effect at all. Because demographics, locations, and cognitive domains of interest vary between studies, it is difficult to determine whether such heterogeneity reflects systematic variation due to study characteristics or whether significant findings are better explained by sampling variance and publication bias. In the present study, we tested for seasonality in 100,113 chess games played by 27,335 players. Not only did we not find a seasonal effect, but we also found no interactions with age, gender, the absolute latitude of the tournament location, and the human development index of a player’s country. Our results suggest that the cognitive capacities required by chess do not vary seasonally, and that there are unlikely to be populations or locations in which it does.

articles.title: Pseudo-Factor Analysis with Item Intercepts
articles.authors: Nigel Guenole
articles.abstract:
We extend the pseudo-factor analysis method by adding a mean structure to obtain pseudo-intercepts. We do so by substituting the
item response mean vector based on human responses with a vector of scalars obtained by projecting item embeddings via dot products
onto semantic intensity axes capturing the difference between low and high scale anchors. Across six scales measuring the trait domains
of the DSM-5 alternative model for personality disorders in occupational settings, the mean correlation between empirical and pseudo-intercepts was r = .54, while the mean correlation between empirical and pseudo-intercepts obtained via deliberate projection onto
randomly incorrect semantic axes was r = −.35. The inclusion of embedding-based mean proxies in pseudo-factor analysis can potentially
enhance scale development and allow mean and covariance structure modeling without empirical data. This may open the possibility of
examining relative item locations, multiple-group pseudo-CFA/SEM modeling, including measurement and structural invariance analyses
across languages and tranformer architectures. Topics such as systematic methods to identify semantic anchors and risks including ‘anchor hacking’ are discussed.

articles.title: A Machine Learning Pipeline to Classify the Type and Severity of Misspecifications in Latent Measurement Models
articles.authors: Melanie Viola Partsch; David Goretzko
articles.abstract: Evaluating and, if necessary, revising factor-analytic latent measurement models is a challenging task. By now, researchers can select from a toolbox of traditional and novel methods to evaluate global and local model fit. However, this toolbox lacks a method to evaluate model fit at an intermediate level facilitating a thoughtful revision process. In this study, we fill this gap with a machine learning (ML) approach to model fit evaluation. Specifically, we introduce an ML pipeline to classify the type and severity of misspecifications in multi-factorial measurement models. To build this pipeline, we trained three ML models that predict a) if the measurement model is correctly specified or if a latent factor, cross-loadings, factor correlations, or residual correlations have been neglected, b) if any, how many cross-loadings have been neglected, and c) if any, how many residual correlations have been neglected. All three ML models performed well overall, with performance drops mainly resulting from misclassifications by ± 1 cross-loading and ± 1–2 residual correlations, respectively. Consequently, the feedback of the ML pipeline must not be implemented in an exact manner. Instead, it points the user to the most likely source of misfit and indicates if the measurement model is not, mildly, moderately, or severely misspecified. This information provides helpful guidance in deciding if and how to revise the measurement model, the measurement instrument, or its underlying theory. We provide an open-access R implementation of the ML pipeline, discuss how to use it within a multi-method approach, and make recommendations on how to use its feedback for the next revision steps.

articles.title: Through Human Eye: Can a Robot Possess a Sense of Self?
articles.authors: Kristina Nikolovska; Jan Pohl; Bernhard Hommel; Arvid Kappas; Francesco Maurelli
articles.abstract: In recent years, there has been significant progress in developing service robots for various settings, prompting a need to examine user--robot interactions. This paper investigates how implementing specific self-characteristics in non-humanoid robots influences user perception. We propose a return to fundamental self-aspects to understand their impact on robot perception. The study examines five fundamental attributes of selfhood (causality, equifinality, behavioral efficiency, learning sensitivity, context sensitivity) and their influence on user perception, utilizing a vehicle-like Duckiebot robot in a controlled experimental setting. By simplifying the complexity of selfhood into manageable traits, we aim to streamline robot development for smoother integration into diverse environments. Our ongoing research aims to contribute to a more comprehensive understanding of human--robot interactions and the development of user-friendly robotic systems that foster positive, trustworthy user experiences.

articles.title: Cultural Worldviews and Environmental Policy Support: Domain-Contingent Associations Across Climate Change and Biodiversity Loss
articles.authors: Aya Takagi; Ryosuke Nakadai
articles.abstract: Social polarisation poses a major challenge to collective climate action. In this context, biodiversity conservation, when aligned with climate objectives, may face less worldview-driven resistance than direct climate policies. However, the links between values of nature and wider cultural worldviews remain poorly understood. We investigated this relationship using a nationwide survey (N = 1,133) in Japan. Individuals with hierarchical worldviews perceived climate change risks as lower and tended to be less supportive of climate change measures than individuals with more egalitarian worldviews. Yet these negative associations were weaker for perceived biodiversity loss risks and for support for climate change-related biodiversity conservation measures, and no direct association was found for stand-alone biodiversity conservation measures. General scientific knowledge was positively associated with support for biodiversity conservation measures, including those related to climate change. Associations between cultural worldviews and policy support differed across climate and biodiversity policy domains.

articles.title: Valence-biased updating differentially influences memory-guided affective ratings.
articles.authors: Ga In Shin; Sarah DuBrow; Vishnu P. Murty
articles.abstract: Understanding how memories of past experiences shape subjective feelings is complicated by the fact that we constantly update our memories. These updates are particularly impactful when individuals are reminded of emotionally positive or negative attributes of the original event. Yet, it remains unclear how such memory updating influences subjective feelings. Here, we investigated how the reactivation of emotional information affects episodic memory, subjective feelings, and their interaction. Across three experiments, participants first learned both positive and negative attributes associated with unfamiliar individuals. Then, they were reminded of a single positive or negative attribute for each individual to reactivate the memory partially. Finally, we reassessed memory for and subjective feelings about each individual’s attributes. In Experiments 1 and 2, these procedures were distributed across three days, while in Experiment 3, they occurred on a single day. Across these three experiments, reminding with negative attributes shifted subjective feelings in a negative direction. Reminded attributes were also better remembered, particularly for negative ones, and changes in subjective feelings were more strongly associated with reminded attributes. However, positive reminders only influenced subjective feelings to change positively when all procedures occurred on the same day. Together, these findings support a model in which memory updating shapes both episodic memory and emotional experience in a valence-dependent manner.

articles.title: From social input to social attention: Parental mood predictability shapes the development of gaze cueing
articles.authors: Dimana V. Atanassova; Marlene Meyer; Sabine Hunnius
articles.abstract:
Gaze following is a fundamental developmental milestone for social cognition. Adults’ attention is reliably cued by gaze shifts but less is known about how gaze cuing emerges in early childhood or what shapes its developmental trajectory. Reduced sensitivity to social input—such as gazes—and differences in the early social environment can affect the development of gaze cueing but the role of these factors remains untested.
We explored the developmental trajectory of the gaze cueing effect (GCE) and the factors shaping it in a large longitudinal sample from the YOUth cohort (N=1928; 5 months, 10 months, 3 years, 6 years). Using static facial stimuli, we found no conclusive evidence of gaze cueing in the first year of life. By 3 years, an early form of the GCE was detectable, with children making more errors on gaze-incongruent trials. By 6 years, the effect was robustly established, yet remained sensitive to characteristics of children’s early social environment.

Contrary to our expectations, reduced social sensitivity indexed by higher callous-unemotional traits did not attenuate the GCE at any age; instead, at 3 years, it was associated with faster overall target detection. Parental mood unpredictability, indexing exposure to an inconsistent social environment, was associated with attenuated gaze cueing at 6 years. In contrast, greater parental mood negativity was associated with slower disengagement from gaze-incongruent targets.

Together, these findings map the developmental trajectory of gaze cueing across early childhood and highlight the role of the parental characteristics as a key factor in shaping early social-cognitive development.

articles.title: Philippe Tissié and Pathological Wanderlust: A Historical Note on the Origin of Exercise Addiction Research
articles.authors: Matthias Weigelt
articles.abstract: Maladaptive and compulsive patterns of exercise behavior – often subsumed under the term exercise addiction – are commonly framed as a proposed form of non substance related behavioral addiction. Research consistently links such patterns to impaired control over exercise, persistent urges to be physically active, and continued engagement despite adverse health and social consequences. The origin of scientific research on exercise addiction is commonly traced back to Baekeland’s seminal study published in 1970. Historical inquiry, however, indicates that scholarly interest in excessive movement behavior emerged much earlier. In particular, the late nineteenth century work of the French physician Philippe Tissié offers an important antecedent. By revisiting Tissié’s descriptions of pathological wanderlust and excessive movement behavior in athletes, this paper positions his observations as a historical precursor to contemporary sport psychological conceptualizations of exercise addiction.

articles.title: When AI Feels like Human: Anthropomorphism, Loneliness, and Problematic Use of Chatbots among School Teachers in Poland
articles.authors: Maciej Ignatowski; Magdalena Duczmal; Marcin Moskalewicz
articles.abstract: The rapid spread of AI chatbots has raised concerns about problematic patterns of use resembling behavioral addictions. This study examined the relationship between problematic AI use, loneliness, and anthropomorphization. Based on a sample of 102 Polish adult AI chatbot users working as school teachers who completed a survey, we found that higher loneliness was associated with stronger anthropomorphization and greater problematic AI use, although the effects were modest. However, the data did not support the mediating role of anthropomorphization. Overall levels of anthropomorphization and social motivation were low, suggesting that these factors may play a limited role in groups that use AI mainly for practical, work-related purposes. Qualitative aspects of engagement may be more informative than time-based indicators alone, highlighting the need to go beyond purely quantitative measures of AI use when assessing problematic engagement.

articles.title: Disinformation in Practice: Threats, Actors, Mechanisms, and Constraints According to Professionals
articles.authors: Grégoire Darcy; Sacha Altay
articles.abstract: How do the professionals charged with fighting disinformation actually understand the problem they are meant to solve? We surveyed 106 French practitioners across military, government, regulatory, and media organizations. They converge on an upstream and offer-driven diagnosis – platforms and amplification structures drive the threat – but describe a field blocked by weak coordination, scarce resources, and fear of censorship accusations. The relationship to science compounds the problem: most practitioners trust peer-reviewed research, yet few engage with it directly, leaving practice disconnected from the evidence base it claims to rely on. Nearly all expect the threat to worsen. Only 13% believe the capacity to respond will catch up.

articles.title: Detecting Response Styles in Visual Analogue Scales
articles.authors: Dominik Vollbracht; Tanja Lischetzke; Mirka Henninger
articles.abstract: Response styles are prevalent in self-report data and may bias parameter estimates. While there are various approaches for detecting response styles in Likert-type data, models for visual analogue scales remain scarce. We propose a novel modeling framework that extends the beta item response model by Noel and Dauvier (2007) to jointly detect extreme response style, midpoint response style, and acquiescent response style. Estimated in a Bayesian framework using Stan, the model performed well in a comprehensive simulation study. Parameter recovery was primarily influenced by the number of (reverse-scored) items and the variance of response styles, whereas sample size had a negligible effect. The model’s practical utility is illustrated using empirical data (Kuhlmann et al., 2017), for which it demonstrated superior model fit compared to the original beta item response model. Overall, the proposed framework provides researchers with a principled approach to account for response styles in visual analogue scale data.

articles.title: What Makes Good Apps Fail or Succeed in the Classroom? The Teacher Support Framework for Integrating Educational Apps (TSF-Apps)
articles.authors: Mirijam Schaaf; Ann-Kathrin Krause; Stephen M. Schueller; Jasmin Breitwieser; Garvin Brod
articles.abstract:
Background: Despite growing access to smartphones and tablets in schools, many educational apps do not succeed in the classroom. Prior research shows that even well-designed and pedagogically grounded tools often fall short when their integration into classroom practice is insufficient. Building on insights from educational psychology and digital health research, we argue that teacher support plays a critical role in determining whether apps become a meaningful part of students’ learning activities. 

Objectives: We propose a new framework, the Teacher Support Framework for Integrating Educational Apps in the Classroom (TSF-Apps), that conceptualizes four key facets where app integration in the classroom can fail: fit, engagement, knowledge, and application. For each facet, we demonstrate how targeted teacher scaffolding can support effective app use and learning. 

Methods: To illustrate its diagnostic and practical value, we apply the framework to a case study of a mobile app designed to foster self-regulated learning in young students. We draw on mixed-methods data (app logs, questionnaire data, and open-ended answers from N = 263 students, Mage = 11.6 years; two teacher interviews) to identify points of failure and corresponding teacher support needs. 

Results and conclusion: The case study demonstrated that the app’s effectiveness was limited by short-lived engagement, shallow understanding of the content, and weak transfer of strategies into everyday learning activities. The findings highlight the need for teacher scaffolding of both technology use and learning content. The proposed framework offers a structured lens for researchers and practitioners seeking to improve the integration of educational apps in classrooms.

articles.title: Translational Literacy Framework (TLF): A Pedagogical Architecture for Cross-Disciplinary Evidence Evaluation and Communication in the AI Era
articles.authors: Caleb Braxton Bragg
articles.abstract: Students graduate from college knowing how to evaluate evidence in their own field but largely unable to evaluate evidence from fields they were not trained in, or to communicate complex findings to audiences who do not share their expertise. This is not a motivation problem. It is a structural one: no course, credential, or framework explicitly teaches this skill. I call this skill translational literacy, the capacity to read, evaluate, and communicate scientific evidence across disciplinary boundaries and across expert-to-public boundaries. This paper introduces the Translational Literacy Framework (TLF), a pedagogical architecture for developing translational literacy at the undergraduate level. The TLF operates through three integrated components: Convergent Triadic Instruction (CTI), in which natural science, social/behavioral science, and library/information science faculty co-deliver instruction in designed convergence; a Modular Disciplinary Architecture (MDA), consisting of portable, discipline-specific instructional modules that can be configured by any institution; and AI Interrogation, a critical stance that positions generative AI as a scientific instrument to be calibrated, pressed, and questioned rather than trusted. I argue that translational literacy is the meta-competency (i.e., a higher-order capacity that integrates and coordinates several lower-order competencies) that connects scientific literacy, information literacy, data literacy, and science communication, adding the cross-disciplinary evaluation layer that none of these provides on its own. The TLF is designed for operationalization through a proposed Certificate in Applied Scientific Communication at Central Connecticut State University, which would be among the first AI-integrated undergraduate scientific communication credentials in U.S. higher education. The framework maps directly onto the Career Readiness Pathway (Bragg & Conway, 2026): the Translational Literacy in Science course is designed to develop career-specific KSAs and core competencies (Pillars 1 and 2), while employer-matched micro-internships provide experiential learning opportunities and career mentorship (Pillars 3 and 4). Empirical validation from a pilot implementation (N = 20, Spring 2027) will be reported separately. The authoritative version of the Translational Literacy Framework (TLF), including licensing terms, is available on the Open Science Framework (OSF) under a Creative Commons Attribution–NonCommercial 4.0 International License.

articles.title: Die 4K im Gewand empirischer Forschung: Eine Antwort auf Albrecht et al. (2025)
articles.authors: Benedikt Wisniewski; Tanja Marie Fritz; Martin Daumiller
articles.abstract: Die sogenannten „4K“ (Kommunikation, Kollaboration, kritisches Denken und Kreativität) spielen eine zentrale Rolle in aktuellen bildungspolitischen Diskursen und werden zunehmend empirisch modelliert. Der Beitrag von Albrecht et al. (2025) beansprucht, Struktur und Förderbarkeit dieser Kompetenzen im schulischen Kontext nachzuweisen. Die vorliegende Replik nimmt diesen Beitrag zum Anlass für eine systematische epistemische und methodische Prüfung. Im Zentrum steht die Frage, inwieweit die zugrunde gelegten Konstrukte theoretisch hinreichend bestimmt, empirisch trennbar und belastbar interpretierbar sind. Die Analyse zeigt, dass die berichteten Befunde die angenommene Struktur der 4K nur eingeschränkt stützen und dass die Prä-Post-Daten aufgrund des Designs keine starken Aussagen über Interventionseffekte erlauben. Am Beispiel von Albrecht et al. (2025) wird damit deutlich, welche besonderen Herausforderungen entstehen, wenn normativ etablierte Kom-petenzmodelle in empirische Mess- und Interventionsforschung überführt werden. Wir plädieren entsprechend für eine klarere Trennung zwischen bildungspolitischer Zielsetzung, theoretischer Modellbildung und empirischer Prüfung.

articles.title: Whoever is Not With Me is Against Me: The ‘Moderate as Out-Group’ Effect
articles.authors: Giulia Maimone; Craig McKenzie
articles.abstract:
Common intuition suggests that expressing moderate views would allow people to appeal to the broadest audience possible. But is that really the case? Do moderates please all sides or please no side? Across five preregistered studies (N = 3,272), we show that people holding a partisan view on a sociopolitical issue perceive moderates (i.e., people disclosing a genuinely non-extreme position) as belonging to the out-group ideology. We find that the ‘moderate as out-group’ effect occurs when sociopolitical issues are moralized and at the same time the opposing side is perceived to be a threat to oneself, close ones, or society at large. We also present evidence that the effect is due to partisans’ perception that moderates lack out-group hate, rather than lack in-group love. In other words, partisans perceive that moderates agree with—or simply don’t condemn—the opposing (immoral and threatening) ideology, rather than disagree with their own. Consistent with this, the ‘moderate as out-group’ effect occurs when the moderate view is framed as pro-both sides, but it is attenuated when it is framed as pro-neither side.


©American Psychological Association, 2026. This paper is not the copy of record and may not exactly replicate the authoritative document published in the APA journal. The final article is available, upon publication, at: 10.1037/xge0001957

articles.title: Emotion Regulation Strategies in Adults With ADHD Tendencies: Structural Roles of Reappraisal and Suppression in Relation to Mental Health
articles.authors: Ayako Nakashita
articles.abstract:
Objective: Emotion dysregulation is common in adults with attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and is closely related to mental health. This study examined whether reappraisal and suppression partly account for the association between ADHD tendencies and mental health, and whether their structural roles differ according to the level of ADHD tendencies.
Method: A web-based survey was conducted with 294 adults aged 18 years and older. ADHD tendencies were assessed using the Adult ADHD Self-Report Scale (ASRS), emotion regulation strategies using the Emotion Regulation Questionnaire (ERQ), and mental health using the K6. Participants were categorized into above- and below-cutoff groups based on ASRS screening scores for multi-group comparison.
Results: Preliminary analyses did not support moderation effects of reappraisal or suppression. Mediation analyses suggested that emotion regulation strategies partly accounted for the association between ADHD tendencies and mental health, particularly through reappraisal. Multi-group structural equation modeling showed that the indirect pathway through reappraisal was relatively consistent across groups, whereas the suppression–mental health path differed significantly between groups and was significant only in the below-cutoff group.
Conclusion: Reappraisal and suppression did not appear to moderate the ADHD–mental health association but were involved in this association as mediating pathways, although their roles differed. The group-specific role of suppression suggests that its association with mental health may vary across levels of ADHD tendencies. These findings indicate that ADHD-related emotion dysregulation may need to be understood not only in terms of habitual strategy use but also in relation to the broader emotional experiences being regulated.

articles.title: Impact of AI on Human Expertise: Canaries in the Coalmine
articles.authors: Andrew J. Waters; Andrea Brancaccio; Shmuel Juni; Fernand Gobet
articles.abstract: Artificial intelligence is increasingly able to perform cognitive tasks that were once thought to require human expertise. This raises the question: what happens to human expertise when AI systems equal or exceed expert human performance? We examine this question from the perspective of chess, a domain in which superhuman AI has existed for more than 25 years. We argue that chess provides not a single “canary in the coalmine,” but a set of canaries for how AI may affect human expertise. Chess shows that superhuman AI emerged earlier than expected, developed through multiple approaches, and can operate through mechanisms that share some functional similarities with human expertise. It also recalibrated assumptions about expert judgment, and challenged the expectation that human + AI will always outperform AI alone. Beyond performance, AI has reshaped chess training and knowledge generation, as engines now guide preparation, opaque AI outputs can be translated into human-understandable concepts, and AI-generated ideas can be taught to grandmasters. Finally, chess illustrates institutional consequences of superhuman AI, especially around cheating, governance, and the changing status of human expertise. We consider how these chess-based canaries may generalize to other domains while also emphasizing limits to generalization.

articles.title: Brain mechanisms for processing systematic sound-to-meaning mappings for concrete and abstract concepts
articles.authors: Greig Ian de Zubicaray; Marko Krsmanovic; Katie Louise McMahon; Valeriya Tolkacheva; Joanne arciuli
articles.abstract: To date, neurobiological accounts of lexical-semantic processing have largely assumed, either explicitly or implicitly, that there is no relationship between the sound of a word and its meaning, despite empirical evidence to the contrary. Recently, multiple representation theories have begun to incorporate non-arbitrary relationships in which a word’s form resembles its meaning (iconicity). However, non-arbitrary form-meaning relationships occur more extensively within languages as statistical regularities between sublexical phonological/phonetic features and aspects of word meaning (systematicity or typicality). The present study investigated whether brain activity during lexical processing reflects statistical regularities between sublexical features and concrete and abstract concepts (i.e., concreteness form typicality). Twenty-one healthy participants completed an event-related functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study while they performed auditory lexical decisions on concrete and abstract words that were highly form-typical. We observed significant activity in a predominantly left-hemisphere network of perisylvian regions previously linked to concrete and abstract word processing. Crucially, the majority of these regions also responded to nonwords constructed with sublexical phonological/phonetic features associated with concreteness, including hub regions proposed to represent amodal conceptual processing. These findings demonstrate that statistical knowledge about non-arbitrary form-meaning mappings constitutes a core component of how concreteness is represented and accessed in the brain. We argue that these results support an account in which concrete and abstract concepts are grounded in the phonetic-acoustic features of language through statistical learning.

articles.title: Seeing, hearing, and feeling causation
articles.authors: Elyse D. Z. Chase; Kevin Smith; Sean Follmer; Tobias Gerstenberg
articles.abstract: How do people decide whether one event caused another? While previous research has focused on visual and auditory cues in causal perception, the role of touch remains underexplored. Here, we investigate how haptic feedback contributes to causal judgments across three psychophysical experiments. In Experiment 1, we introduced force-based haptic feedback to a visual launching paradigm and found that haptic information increased causal judgments compared to visual feedback alone. Experiment 2 combined vision, audio, force-based haptics, and vibrotactile haptics, revealing that additional sensory cues increase causal judgments with diminishing returns—the largest benefit comes from adding a second modality. In Experiment 3, we examined how both the number and physical realism of multisensory cues affect causal perception, finding that both factors boosted causal judgments. We present a Bayesian multimodal inference model that captures human judgments by integrating visual, auditory, and haptic information based on their relative timing, uncertainty, and realism. Taken together, these experiments show that haptic information contributes to causal judgments by shaping the multisensory evidence observers use when deciding whether one event physically caused another, including how realistic and physically coherent the event appears. More broadly, we find that temporal alignment and cross-modal coherence are key constraints for how multisensory evidence shapes causal judgments, with implications for virtual reality, robotics, and human-computer interaction systems.

articles.title: Autumn leaves in colourful tone or narrow streets of cobblestone – is virtual nature superior for well-being when compared to a virtual old-town environment?
articles.authors: Gerhard Reese; Corinna Erbel; Stefanie Kukariodis; Anne S Kunik; Hanna Pfeifer; Nina von der Linde; Jan-Marius Wehinger; Claudia Menzel
articles.abstract: Immersive virtual reality environments allow virtual nature experiences that can have effects on well-being similar to real nature experiences. Most research on the restorative effects of both real and virtual nature relies on comparisons between green (or blue) nature and grey modern urban scenes so that aesthetics rather than urbanity might drive these effects. Therefore, in the current laboratory experiment, we tested whether an immersive virtual forest walk improved indicators of well-being more strongly than a serene virtual town walk. Eighty participants either navigated through a virtual autumn forest scenario or a historic city centre scenario. Before and after the intervention, we measured participants’ heart rate, positive and negative affect, and subjective vitality as indicators of well-being. After the intervention, we additionally assessed perceived restoration outcome. Results suggest that the virtual nature experience resulted in stronger well-being on most indicators compared to the town experience. We suggest that more systematic variation of urban VR environments is required.

articles.title: The Why Behind the How: Motives Predict Emotion Regulation Strategy Use in Everyday Life
articles.authors: Valentina Bianchi; Tyler Thorne; Elise Katherine Kalokerinos; Felicity Angelopoulos; Marina Milyavskaya
articles.abstract: Theories suggest that capturing people’s motives (the ‘why’) for regulating allows for a better understanding of how people choose to regulate their emotions. Yet, little empirical research systematically examines the link between motives and regulation strategies. Addressing this gap, we conducted an experience-sampling study to: 1) capture the prevalence of a wide range of regulatory motives (hedonic, contrahedonic, instrumental) in everyday life and (2) test whether motives predict the selection of six common regulation strategies, controlling for affect intensity. Data were collected in Australia in 2024, from an adult community sample (NID=303;  Nobs=3496; Mage = 26.11; 80% women, mostly identifying as East Asian and South-East Asian). Hedonic motives were most frequent, but instrumental motives were also common. Regulatory motives showed distinct strategy selection profiles: hedonic motives were linked with greater distraction and cognitive reappraisal, whereas contrahedonic motives were rare, and only predicted greater rumination. Different instrumental motives were differentially associated with the use of specific strategies. Only cognitive strategies (reappraisal and rumination) served multiple motives — they were significantly associated with hedonic, contrahedonic (rumination), personal performance, social, epistemic, and eudaimonic motives. By investigating the momentary association between a broad range of motives and regulation strategies, this work confirms that emotion regulation strategies are selected not only based on what people feel, but also why they regulate. Future empirical work should explicitly consider individuals’ regulatory motives, rather than assuming regulatory effectiveness based on hedonic motives.

articles.title: From structure to sequence: a Multinomial Processing Tree model of syntactic encoding
articles.authors: Jeremy Yeaton; Grant M. Walker; Danielle Fahey; William Matchin; Julius Fridriksson; Gregory Hickok
articles.abstract: Two-stage models of grammatical encoding posit that sentence production unfolds in two sequential steps: the construction of a hierarchical syntactic structure, followed by its linearization into a sequence suitable for articulation. While widely accepted in psycho- and neurolinguistics, such models lack formal computational implementations. Here, we introduce a novel Multinomial Processing Tree (MPT) model that operationalizes this two-stage framework to explain syntactic error patterns in individuals with aphasia. Drawing on discourse samples annotated for distinct error types, we fit an MPT model to estimate individual abilities at each processing stage. These ability estimates correlated with observed error rates and localized to distinct neural substrates: hierarchical encoding ability was linked to the posterior superior temporal sulcus and parietal cortex, linearization to posterior inferior frontal regions, and omission-related processes to more dorsal frontal regions and the underlying white matter. Our findings support a neurocomputational dissociation between hierarchical and linear stages of grammatical encoding, aligning with prior lesion-symptom mapping and theoretical accounts of expressive agrammatism and paragrammatism. This work represents the first computational instantiation of a two-stage model of syntactic production, bridging formal modeling and lesion analysis to advance our understanding of the architecture and breakdown of grammatical encoding.

articles.title: Heartbeat Counting Task Performance in Alcohol Use Disorder: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
articles.authors: Nicolás Guillermo González; Luisa Göde; Lucia Alba-Ferrara
articles.abstract: Interoception has been proposed as a key process in alcohol use disorder (AUD), and several studies using the Heartbeat Counting Task (HCT) have reported lower task performance in individuals with AUD than in healthy controls (HC). However, concerns about the construct validity of the HCT complicate the interpretation of these findings. This systematic review and meta-analysis aimed to quantify the magnitude and consistency of group differences in HCT performance between adults with AUD and HC, without assuming that the task provides a process-pure measure of cardiac interoceptive accuracy (IAcc). Eligible studies compared adults diagnosed with AUD with HC and reported sufficient HCT data to compute standardized mean differences. Searches were conducted in PubMed/MEDLINE, PsycINFO, Embase, and Web of Science, last searched on November 4, 2025. Six studies, including 314 individuals with AUD and 324 HC, were included. Study quality was indexed using an adapted Newcastle–Ottawa Scale. Standardized mean differences, expressed as Hedges’ g, were pooled using a random-effects model. Results indicated a moderate-to-large negative pooled effect (g = −0.79, 95% CI [−0.96, −0.63]), reflecting lower HCT performance in AUD. All studies showed negative effect sizes, although the small evidence base warrants caution. These findings identify lower HCT performance as a consistent task-level behavioral difference in AUD, rather than direct evidence of reduced cardiac IAcc, and underscore the need to determine whether this difference relates to clinically relevant features of the disorder. The review received no specific external funding and was preregistered in PROSPERO (ID = CRD420251173904).

articles.title: Adolescent Disclosure Strongly Predicts General Knowledge and Weakly Predicts Alcohol Knowledge: Revisiting How Parents Learn About Adolescent Drinking
articles.authors: Sophia Helena Blyth; Savannah Boele; Robert Laird; William E. Pelham
articles.abstract:
Purpose: Where do parents get information about their adolescent’s alcohol use? The prevailing assumption in the field is that adolescent disclosure (teens voluntarily sharing information with parents) is the primary driver of parental knowledge. However, this assumption relies on studies that have measured adolescent disclosure and parent knowledge of general information, not about alcohol use in particular. The present study addressed two key questions: 1) Does parental general knowledge predict parental alcohol knowledge? and 2) Does adolescent disclosure predict parental alcohol knowledge? 
Methods: We tested these questions using longitudinal data from 176 adolescent-parent dyads who completed biweekly surveys across one year. Parental alcohol knowledge was operationalized as parent-adolescent agreement on reports of adolescent alcohol use, assessed via polynomial regression in a multilevel framework. We focused on within-person analyses, as causal processes related to disclosure primarily occur within families. 
Results: Results indicated that parental alcohol knowledge was moderate to high across waves. However, parental general knowledge showed only a weak association with parental alcohol knowledge, and adolescent general disclosure showed only a weak association with parental alcohol knowledge -- despite disclosure being a strong predictor of parental general knowledge, as found in prior work.  
Discussion: These findings challenge the field's current model of where parents get knowledge about alcohol use. Parents may acquire knowledge about alcohol use through mechanisms distinct from those by which they acquire general knowledge. Future research should develop alcohol-specific disclosure measures and identify the pathways through which parents come to know about adolescent alcohol use.

articles.title: Computational signatures of exertion and rest underlie moment-to-moment dynamics of subjective perceptions of effort and fatigue
articles.authors: Tanja Müller; Joseph Milton; Masud Husain; Matthew A J Apps
articles.abstract: Everyday we perform tasks that make us feel fatigued. Theoretical accounts predict that fatigue not only develops due to exertion of effort but also increases how effortful the same action will feel subsequently. However, to date there has been no formalised computational account of subjective perceptions of fatigue and effort, with few studies measuring these sensations directly or quantifying their moment-to-moment changes. Here, across three experiments, participants were required to exert different levels of physical effort (grip force below maximum capacity) to obtain rewards, rating on each trial how effortful they found exerting force (Experiment 1) or how fatigued they felt (Experiments 2 and 3). Across studies, ratings of fatigue and perception of effort increased over time, but also fluctuated on a trial-by-trial basis as a function of both effort exerted and rest. A computational model of fatigue, comprising a recoverable component (with fatigue reducing during rest) and an unrecoverable one (in which it only increases through effort exerted) successfully accounted for subjective responses. It was best able to explain momentary changes in both fatigue and effort perception. This computational model provides insights into the brain mechanisms underpinning the close, dynamic relationship between sensations of fatigue and effort.

articles.title: Dialectical Behavior Coaching to Prevent Activist Burnout
articles.authors: Monika Neff Lind; Alexis Adams-Clark
articles.abstract: When political aggression toward immigrants, trans people, and disabled people in the US intensified in January 2025, we struggled with how to help. We called our representatives, donated to progressive efforts, and attended rallies. Those actions were meaningful and rewarding, and we began thinking about how to leverage our clinical training to increase our impact. We determined that adopting a support role for frontline activists was our best option. To maximize our effectiveness, we explored the fit between DBT skills and activist burnout. In this paper, we share our insights and actions.

articles.title: Application of Linear Control Theory to a Simple Cognitive Model of Controlled Processing: A Case Study
articles.authors: Clay B. Holroyd
articles.abstract: Cognitive effort is a defining feature of cognitive control but theories about its origin and purpose have long been controversial. Recently, it has been proposed that cognitive effort can be understood using linear control theory (LCT), a mathematical formalism developed in the engineering literature to control dynamical systems. On this view, effort levels reflect the energy required by the cognitive control system to support representational states that deviate from the intrinsic dynamics of the cognitive system. Although LCT holds promise for elucidating a range of effort-related processes such as working memory and task switching, the technique is likely unfamiliar to many cognitive psychologists. Therefore, to introduce the approach to a broader audience, here we apply LCT to a classic computational model of cognitive control: Cohen et al.’s (1990) artificial neural network model of the Stroop task. In so doing, this study serves as a tutorial on the application of control theoretic principles to models of cognitive control and their associated effort costs.

articles.title: Pregnancy reshapes the interoceptive and sensorimotor characterization of abstract and concrete concepts: A rating study
articles.authors: Giuseppina Porciello; Laura Barca; Chiara Fini; cristina trentini; cristiano violani; Anna M. Borghi
articles.abstract:
Recent theories of embodied cognition suggest that conceptual knowledge is grounded not only in external sensory experiences but also in internal bodily states. Interoception, the sensing of physiological signals from within the body, has been identified as a key modality for representing abstract concepts. However, whether this grounding is dynamically modulated by significant physiological transitions remains unexplored. The present study investigates how the profound bodily changes occurring during pregnancy influence the semantic characterization of abstract and concrete concepts.
Eighty-one (40 pregnant, 41 age-matched non-pregnant controls) women performed a rating task on 40 words (20 abstract, 20 concrete) across several dimensions: abstractness, concreteness, emotionality, valence, exteroception, interoception, and Body-Object Interaction (BOI). Participants also completed clinical and interoceptive sensibility self-reported questionnaires (MAIA, GAD-7, TAS-20, BDI-II).
Results confirmed that abstract concepts are characterized by higher interoceptive strength and emotionality, while concrete concepts are more strongly associated with exteroception and BOI, in line with current literature. Crucially, however, pregnancy significantly modulated these representations: pregnant women rated abstract concepts as more interoceptive, emotional and more concrete compared to the control group. Furthermore, pregnant women attributed higher BOI scores to concrete concepts, suggesting an increased sensitivity to potential physical interactions between their changing body and the environment. Hence, our results reveal that, with concrete words, pregnant women pay more attention to their bodily experience of external objects; with abstract words, they tend to pay more attention to their inner and affective bodily signals. 
These findings provide novel evidence for the embodied and dynamic nature of conceptual knowledge, showing that conceptual representations are flexibly modulated in response to the individual's current physiological and interoceptive state.

articles.title: Beyond the Resume: Comparing the Predictive Power of Personality and Employment History in Employee Turnover Risk Using Explainable AI
articles.authors: Mohammad Javad Veradi Esfahani; Ehsan Rezayat; Jafar Adibi
articles.abstract: Employee turnover prediction is a long-standing challenge in organizational research; however, the vast majority of the literature has focused on post-hire data, and relatively few studies have modeled the predictive utility of pre-hire personality features. This study used a comparative modeling framework to evaluate the predictive capability of personality traits versus early-career behavioral history. Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) classifiers were trained on a dataset of 2,126 job seekers using a nested cross-validation procedure, and the model's decision logic was interpreted using the SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) framework. Results demonstrate that a model trained only on personality and demographic data achieves predictive performance (AUC = 0.896) comparable to a model based on behavioral history (AUC = 0.894) for predicting turnover risk, while the model trained on combined data achieved the highest performance (AUC = 0.914). Moreover, the Explainable AI (XAI) analysis revealed complex, non-linear contribution patterns of personality features, such as a V-shaped effect for Agreeableness and threshold effects for Neuroticism and Conscientiousness. These findings suggest that pre-hire personality assessments may serve as an alternative or supplement to resume-based work history in screening scenarios.

articles.title: Orienting selective attention within long-term contextual memories
articles.authors: Melinda Sabo; William Narhi-Martinez; Nahid Zokaei; Nora Roüast; Anna C. Nobre
articles.abstract: Selective attention is central to long-term memory retrieval, yet how attention selects and prioritizes relevant memory contents remains underexplored. Well-controlled experimental designs aimed at isolating and systematically manipulating attentional orienting in long-term memory would greatly advance this cause. The current study addresses this gap. Building on retrocue designs from working-memory research, we introduce procedures to investigate how selective attention operates within contextual long-term memory. Across three pre-registered experiments (n = 235), participants learned associations between scenes and the identities and locations of two objects. In a subsequent Selective Retrieval task, participants viewed the studied scenes and later identified scene-associated objects from an array. Spatial cues directed internal attention to the learned location of the object to be probed, or neutral cues offered no spatial information. Informative cues yielded significant retrieval benefits and costs relative to neutral cues. Benefits emerged regardless of whether cues appeared before or after scene presentation and were fully expressed even with very short retrieval intervals. The approach offers a flexible method for isolating and manipulating attention functions in long-term memory, enabling future investigation of the psychological and neural mechanisms governing selective memory access.

articles.title: Exploring the Use of Multiple Imputation for Handling Missing Covariates in Meta-regression with Dependent Effect Sizes
articles.authors: Jihyun Lee; S. Natasha Beretvas; Brian T Keller
articles.abstract: Meta-analysts frequently encounter missing covariate values, which can complicate valid estimation of meta-regression models. In practice, missing data are managed often through ad hoc deletion approaches, which can reduce the validity of statistical inferences. More advanced missing data handling approaches such as multiple imputation (MI) remain underutilized, particularly in meta-analyses with dependent effect sizes within studies. This study expands the use of MI techniques for handling missing covariates in such contexts. Specifically, this study introduces adapted multilevel MI approaches that are expected to better accommodate the structure of meta-analytic data with dependent effect sizes and associated model. The study presents Monte Carlo computer simulations that compare the performance of different MI techniques including single-level and multilevel agnostic MI methods as well as multilevel substantive model-based MI and ad hoc deletion approaches. The results generally supported the use of the MI approach over deletion approaches when the dependent structure is well specified in the imputation procedure. This study demonstrates the feasibility of the use of MI techniques and underscores the importance of incorporating the hierarchical structure into the meta-regression model when analyzing dependent effect sizes with missing covariate values. Implications and directions for future research are discussed.

articles.title: Risk of Becoming ‘NEET’ (Not in Education, Employment, or Training) Among Students with Special Educational Needs and Disabilities in England
articles.authors: Elizabeth Weir; Simon White; Sarah Louise Griffiths; Mariann Kovacs; Carrie Allison; Simon Baron-Cohen
articles.abstract:
Importance: Becoming NEET is associated with unemployment and poorer health, but no large-scale, recent studies estimate risks across SEND students broadly.
Objectives:  Quantifying risks of becoming NEET among students with and without SEND and assessing whether ‘Raising the Minimum Participation Age’ legislation (RPA) reduces risks.
Design: This observational study used fixed and mixed-effects models to estimate risk of becoming NEET among students with and without SEND via lifetime educational records (aged 5-18years).
Setting: A population-based cohort of >90% of children in England’s National Pupil Database.
Participants: 3.9 million students who began primary school between 1998-2005; 43,580 students were excluded due to missing or duplicated data.
Exposure: SEND status, by labelling of needs and further educational support (Statement/EHCP; Education, Health, and Care Plan).
Main Outcomes and Measures: Becoming NEET during adolescence.
Results: SEND and Unclassified SEND students were more likely to be male, persistently absent, excluded from school, and to have lower socioeconomic status than students without SEND. Unclassified SEND students were also less likely to be white or speak English than students without SEND. After accounting for sociodemographic and educational factors, all types of SEND students were more likely to become NEET than students without SEND, including SEND with Statement/EHCP (OR:1.34; 95%CI:1.31—1.38), SEND without Statement/EHCP (OR:1.80; 95%CI:1.77—1.83), Unclassified SEND with Statement/EHCP (OR:2.54; 95%CI:2.33—2.76), and Unclassified SEND without Statement/EHCP (OR:1.53; 95%CI:1.50—1.56). In the same models, students in later cohorts were relatively less likely to become NEET (Cohort 7 OR:0.36; 95%CI:0.35-0.37); interactions between cohort and SEND were largely non-significant. 
Conclusions and Relevance: SEND students have increased risks of educational disengagement across all types of support, with 22% of SEND and 15% of Unclassified SEND students becoming NEET compared to 7% of students without SEND. This suggests that students perceived to have lesser or different needs still have increased risks. Students who receive more support appear to have lower risks of disengagement, though this may reflect other support/advocacy that inherently lowers risks. The RPA and other educational legislation may have reduced risks of disengagement for all students to age 18, improving outcomes among students with and without SEND proportionally. Targeted educational policies should be implemented to address vulnerability and intersectionality among SEND students.

articles.title: Reliability of Passive Sensing Data: A Multi-Method Evaluation in Depression
articles.authors: Anna M Langener; Ahhyun Lee; Sebastian Castro-Alvarez; Elizabeth Webb Lampe; Nicholas Enbar-Salo; Anya Ramrakhiani; Ceyhun Olcan; Hannah Dorris; George Price; Michael V. Heinz
articles.abstract: Passive data are increasingly used to capture continuous and unobtrusive information in psychological research. While digital markers based on step count, time at home, distance traveled, and sleep are frequently associated with depression-related and other psychological constructs, the reliability of passive data is rarely evaluated, potentially limiting credibility. In this paper, we evaluated the reliability of five depression-related constructs–sleep disturbance, activity level, affective dysregulation, behavioral inactivation, and social withdrawal– using three approaches: internal consistency, temporal stability, and inter-rater agreement. Analyses were conducted across daily, weekly, and monthly aggregation in 294 clinically depressed individuals over 90 days. Results revealed heterogeneity across constructs, measures, and timescales. Between-person internal consistency was generally high, indicating that passive measures can robustly differentiate individuals (mean consistency = .88, range = .32–1). Activity emerged as the most reliable construct, showing high between-person (mean = .92) and within-person (mean = .75) consistency, particularly at daily and weekly timescales. In contrast, constructs such as sleep, behavioral inactivation, and social withdrawal showed greater variability and often low within-person reliability, suggesting that their measures capture distinct constructs. Temporal stability increased with longer aggregation windows: while day-to-day behavior fluctuated considerably (mean intra class correlation (ICC) = .37), stability was moderate weekly (mean ICC = .64) and even higher monthly (mean ICC = .78). Lastly, device-specific differences highlight the need to account for measurement effects (Steps (Garmin vs phone): r = .52). These findings underscore a critical yet often overlooked aspect of passive sensing research: reliability should be systematically evaluated when constructing and interpreting passive measures.

articles.title: Leveraging AI for Automatic Item Generation for Psychological Scales
articles.authors: Xijuan Zhang; Kentaro Suzuki; Kai Wen Zhou
articles.abstract: This study examined how large language models (LLMs) can be used for automated item generation (AIG) in psychological scale development, focusing on how prompting strategies and LLM brands and versions influence item quality for both an established and a novel construct. Using three prompting conditions across eight LLMs from four major providers, we generated AI items and evaluated them through expert review, AI-psychometric analyses, and lexical diversity metrics. The findings highlight both the potential and limitations of AIG. Many AI-generated items were clear, concise, and theoretically meaningful, and for the novel construct, some items captured nuanced facets beyond the researcher’s initial conceptualization. At the same time, other items were overly general or tapped unintended dimensions, underscoring the need for careful human screening.  Prompt engineering was the strongest determinant of item quality, requiring deliberate design to avoid reproducing existing scale items and to achieve the desired balance of specificity and conciseness. Overall, AIG appears to be a useful tool for early-stage scale development when paired with thoughtful prompt design and human oversight.

articles.title: Emotion Regulation in the Daily Lives of Women With and Without Eating Disorders: An Ecological Momentary Assessment Study
articles.authors: Sarah Elizabeth Racine; Ege Bicaker; Alexia Miller; Vittoria Trolio; Lisa Yujia Zhu
articles.abstract: Objective: Emotion regulation difficulties are known risk/maintenance factors for eating disorders (EDs). Few studies have investigated use of adaptive/maladaptive emotion regulation strategies in people with EDs, especially using ecologically valid data. This study: 1) compared stressor endorsement and severity, use of adaptive and maladaptive emotion regulation strategies, and perceived helpfulness of these strategies among women with and without EDs; 2) investigated whether adaptive and maladaptive emotion regulation strategy use and perceived helpfulness predicted engagement in ED behaviors among women with EDs. Methods: Participants were 188 women with diverse EDs and 49 women with no ED history. They completed five signal-contingent surveys per day for 14 days that asked about stressors, emotion regulation strategies, purging, and maladaptive exercise. Event-contingent surveys completed after eating assessed binge eating and restriction. Results: Compared to women without EDs, women with EDs reported a greater number of stressors, greater stress severity, less adaptive strategy use, more maladaptive strategy use, and less perceived helpfulness from adaptive strategies. Using an adaptive strategy and perceiving it as more helpful were associated with a lower likelihood of subsequent binge eating. Using distraction was associated with future restriction, while perceiving distraction as unhelpful predicted future maladaptive exercise. None of the within-person results survived Benjamini-Hochberg false discovery rate correction. Discussion: Women with EDs have more stress in daily life and use less effective strategies to regulate it, which could help explain development/maintenance of EDs. However, few relations between adaptive/maladaptive strategy use and subsequent ED behaviors among women with EDs were detected.

articles.title: People Offload Empathy to AI
articles.authors: Madhulika Shastry; Kurt Gray
articles.abstract: Giving empathy is good, but providing emotional support is hard work. Empathic AI gives cheap and easy support, but may be ethically costly. When AI provides empathy, people may feel less compelled to support others. Four studies provide evidence for “empathy offloading:” when people see AI as capable of empathy, they become less willing to empathize with the suffering of others, even without realizing it. When AI seems more capable of empathy people—both with correlations (Study 1) and causal experiments (Study 2)—people report more empathy offloading. When people learn that someone has received emotional support from large language models (LLMs), they engage less with others’ mental states (Study 3) and offer fewer empathic responses (Study 4). Empathic AI might provide low-cost, easily accessible emotional support, but it risks fraying the ethical and emotional commitments we make to other humans. When someone can go cry to AI, why bother getting splashed with their tears?

articles.title: Scaling cognitive science with classroom games for learning mathematics
articles.authors: Joshua T. Dean; Moira Rose Dillon; Esther Duflo; Harini Kannan; Elizabeth Spelke
articles.abstract: We report two randomized evaluations of educational interventions targeting children in low-income neighborhoods of Delhi, India. Four math games, played in social groups, associated early emerging and universally intuitive concepts of number and geometry with the mathematical language, symbols, and operations taught in Delhi government schools. The first experiment, conducted in 231 NGO-run preschool classrooms (1,986 children) showed that the games enhanced children’s mastery of mathematics both immediately after the intervention and a year later, after children’s first year of primary school. The second experiment, conducted in 141 kindergarten and first-grade classrooms in government schools (2,828 children), with no extra personnel, showed that the games remained effective when played cooperatively by larger groups of children, led by their regular teachers, during time allotted for math instruction. These experiments show how insights from basic research on children’s mathematical intuitions can be leveraged to design ready-to-scale interventions for the foundational years.

articles.title: Interpreting conditionals: Cross-linguistic evidence for an asymmetry between perfected and biscuit readings
articles.authors: Paolo Morosi; Nikoleta Pantelidou; Evelina Leivada; Elena Pagliarini
articles.abstract: Conditional statements often convey meanings that go beyond their truth-conditional se-mantics. For instance, the standard conditional If you mow the lawn, I’ll give you €50 is frequently strengthen into a biconditional, “perfected” interpretation, whereby mowing the lawn is the only way to receive the money. By contrast, a biscuit conditional like If John is hungry, there is pizza in the oven conveys that there is pizza in the oven regardless of John’s hunger, with the if-clause merely establishing the relevance conditions for the as-sertion. Although both interpretations are extensively discussed in theoretical linguistics, their relative cognitive accessibility and cross-linguistic robustness remain poorly under-stood. We address this gap by evaluating how 313 adult native speakers of Catalan, Eng-lish, Italian, and Spanish interpret conditionals in contexts that force them to rely on pragmatic inference, rather than truth-functional logic. Across all four languages, perfect-ed readings were endorsed at very high rates (88–93%), whereas biscuit interpretations are accepted only about half of the time (50–62%). These findings provide large-scale cross-linguistic evidence for a stable asymmetry between the two readings. Based on both group-level and individual response patterns, we further argue that speakers default to interpreting conditionals as expressing a dependency between antecedent and consequent, which is readily strengthened into a biconditional reading. Biscuit interpretations, by con-trast, arise only when this default interpretation is overridden through additional pragmatic reasoning.

articles.title: Probing Monetary Gain and Loss as Affective Signals for Adaptive Control
articles.authors: Peter Czingraber; Natalija Dokic; Julianna Harangozo; Dianovics Dominik; Balazs Aczel; Miklos Bognar
articles.abstract: The congruency sequence effect (CSE) is a robust phenomenon, yet whether affective signals drive this adaptation remains unclear. The affective signal hypothesis proposes that it is the negative affect induced by conflict, rather than conflict itself, that serves as the learning signal for control adaptation. However, prior studies testing this hypothesis often bear methodological limitations, including blockwise mood inductions, performance-contingent feedback, and confounds related to feature repetition and contingency learning, ultimately yielding mixed findings in the field. The present registered report addresses these issues using a confound-minimised prime-probe paradigm in which performance non-contingent monetary gain or loss signals are displayed at the trial level, indicated by the colour of the prime stimuli. Bayesian data analysis will be conducted to assess the potential modulatory effects of emotionally laden stimuli on control adaptation. In accordance with the affective signal hypothesis, we predict that monetary loss will enhance CSE relative to neutral trials, while monetary gain will reduce it. Manipulation checks using visual analogue scales will verify whether the monetary conditions elicited the expected affective response.

articles.title: Individual differences in physiological adaptation correlate with cardioceptive precision in the heart rate discrimination task
articles.authors: Guido Maria Caruso; Mateo Leganes-Fonteneau
articles.abstract:
Interoception, the process through which the organism senses internal bodily signals, is most commonly studied across the cardiac domain, yet the physiological basis of cardioception remains only partially understood. Moreover, most research has assessed cardiac interoception at rest, neglecting its dynamic nature.
Here, we used the Heart Rate Discrimination (HRD) task to examine individual differences in cardiac physiology associated with interoceptive precision, particularly through ethanol-induced physiological modulation. In a double-blind within-subjects design, 36 participants completed the HRD at baseline and after consuming an alcohol or placebo beverage. Cardiac indices such as high-frequency and 0.1 Hz heart rate variability (HRV), heart rate baroreflex sensitivity (HR-BRS), and mean heart rate, were recorded before and after beverage administration. 
Preregistered baseline analyses showed no associations between baseline cardiac physiology and HRD precision. Exploratory analyses revealed selective associations for other HRD metrics: HF HRV correlated negatively with metacognitive sensitivity, while interoceptive threshold correlated negatively with mean heart rate and positively with HR-BRS. Crucially, preregistered mixed models
showed that within-subject increases in 0.1 Hz HRV positively correlate with interoceptive precision across both alcohol and placebo sessions.
Our findings show differential physiological pathways feeding into different components of cardioception, as measured with the HRD. Additionally, the main effect of 0.1Hz HRV modulation on cardioceptive precision highlights the need for measuring interoceptive processes under physiological change.

articles.title: Pupil size reveals the cognitive load involved in processing rhythmic stimuli of various tempi and complexity
articles.authors: Elena Fontaine; Connor Spiech; Valentin Bégel
articles.abstract: The processing of rhythmic information is essential in many human behaviors such as speech, music, and movement coordination. According to dynamical system theories, attention aligns with the temporal structure of external stimuli through neural oscillations, facilitating perception and action. Pupillometry provides a non-invasive index of cognitive attention and mental effort, making it a promising tool to study rhythmic processing. In this study, we investigated how tempo relative to individuals’ spontaneous motor tempo (SMT) and rhythmic complexity (strong vs. weak metric structure) interact to modulate the cognitive load during rhythm perception and synchronization. Twenty-six adults were asked to either passively listen to or actively synchronize their finger tapping with auditory sequences while their pupil size was continuously recorded. As predicted, synchronization led to greater pupil dilation than passive listening. Pupil sizes were larger while processing faster rhythms compared to rhythms at slower tempi than one’s SMT. Critically, the effect of tempo on pupil size varied depending on rhythmic complexity and participants’ synchronization performance, suggesting that the pupil dilation reflects the capacity to achieve synchronization. These findings highlight the value of pupillometry for capturing dynamic attentional engagement in rhythm processing and offer new insights for fields such as neurorehabilitation, musical training, and the study of motor coordination.

articles.title: GenAI in the Lives of Young Adults: Exploring Motivations and Mental Health
articles.authors: Anne J. Maheux; Chelly Maes; Benjamin Buck
articles.abstract: Background. Young people have rapidly adopted generative artificial intelligence (genAI) technology, yet little is known about how genAI use relates to mental health. This observational study examined associations between genAI use motivations—emotional support, task automation, learning/exploration, and dating/sexuality—and internalizing symptoms, as well as sociodemographic differences. Methods. U.S. young adults (N = 1003; ages 18–25; 56.3% women, 39.4% men, 4.3% another gender) completed an online survey. Path models tested associations between genAI motivations and mental health, with comparisons by gender, race/ethnicity, and SES. Results. Men, Black youth, and higher SES youth used genAI more and for more purposes. Using genAI for emotional support was linked to higher depressive (s = .22–.29; ps < .004) and anxiety symptoms (s = .22–.30; ps < .008), whereas using genAI for learning and exploration was associated with lower symptoms of depression (s = -.20 – -.40; ps < .008), anxiety (s = -.26 – -.26; ps < .003), and loneliness (s = -.28 – -.42; ps < .001). Multiple group comparisons revealed gender-specific patterns: among women, task automation and dating/sexuality motivations were related to poorer mental health (s = .12–.19; ps < .05). Among men, emotional support motivations predicted greater loneliness ( = .33; p < .001). No significant moderation effects were observed by race/ethnicity or SES. Conclusions. Whereas learning/exploration may be adaptive, reliance on genAI for relational support may exacerbate (or reflect pre-existing) internalizing symptoms, with distinct vulnerabilities by gender. Longitudinal research is needed to clarify temporal pathways and inform interventions.

articles.title: Coming of Age with Generative AI: The High Stakes of Automating Adolescent Development
articles.authors: Anne J. Maheux; Chelly Maes
articles.abstract: Generative artificial intelligence (genAI) is rapidly transforming the context in which adolescents develop, presenting both opportunities and risks. Adolescence is a sensitive life period, when the accomplishment of key psychosocial developmental tasks—including social belonging, emotion regulation, autonomy, and identity formation—has lifelong consequences. This perspective synthesizes interdisciplinary research on how adolescents’ use of genAI chatbots like ChatGPT may have unique impacts during this life stage. Although genAI may provide low-stakes opportunities to simulate social interactions, scaffold emotional regulation, and support identity exploration in some contexts, it also threatens to displace or thwart essential developmental experiences. Risks include increasing social isolation, undermining distress tolerance, distorting expectations for human relationships, compromising identity and purpose development, and exposing youth to biased, inaccurate, or sexually inappropriate content. We call for a developmental science framework to guide future research, technology design, and policy. Longitudinal studies are needed—especially those that examine specific use cases, address all domains of development holistically, account for varying levels of vulnerability across stages and populations of adolescence, leverage interdisciplinary and academic–industry partnerships, and incorporate the perspectives of youth. As adolescents increasingly integrate genAI into their daily lives, we must urgently address how these tools are shaping their transition to adulthood.

articles.title: Cross-Sensory Interaction in Immersion: Critiquing the Two Competitor Model
articles.authors: Mike Richardson; Iain Donald Gilchrist; Danaë Stanton Fraser
articles.abstract: Immersion is a widely experienced yet conceptually contested phenomenon, particularly within Human–Computer Interaction. The Two-Competitor Model proposes that immersion arises from shifts in attentional focus between actual and induced realities. Although the model provides a testable framework for quantifying immersive experiences, it assumes these realities compete along orthogonal dimensions which overlooks instances in which sensory input from the actual reality can enhance, rather than reduce, immersion. This position paper examines three concise examples to demonstrate how cross-sensory interactions can increase the salience of sensory streams in the actual reality while simultaneously supporting the induced reality.
